Data Analysis Investigation Deliverable: a measured diagnosis, not a fix

Why does the dispatcher silently stop launching children?

Children pile up pending, the dispatcher loop launches nothing, and the log stays empty. This analysis reads the run-state data to explain why — and to quantify the gap between what the status API reports and what the database actually holds.

Key finding

A live capture showed the API reporting 2 running while the database held 7 — every slot full. The slots are occupied by zombies, not workers.

The reframe

The right question isn't "is the loop dead?" It's "how many slots does the database think are occupied, and by what?"

01

What we're trying to find out

One question carries the whole investigation: why does the cast-server dispatcher enqueue children as pending yet never launch them, with nothing in the log? The output is a measured finding, not a patch.

The symptom is alarming and quiet at once — pending children accumulate, the dispatcher loop logs nothing, and HTTP latency stays flat and healthy.

That flat latency is a clue, not just reassurance: a hung event loop would stall HTTP too. Healthy latency therefore rules out a dead loop and points the investigation elsewhere.

The decisive move is to distrust the GET /api/agents/runs?status=running view and read the agent_runs table directly — because the two disagree, and the size of that disagreement is the entire point.

02

What the data shows

The diagnosis the data supports: the slots are full of zombies, not workers, and the status API is under-reporting it. The load-bearing number is the gap between what the API says and what the database actually holds.

2
running, per the status API
vs
7
running, straight from the database

Seven of seven slots — full saturation. The only trustworthy count is the one taken directly from the DB: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM agent_runs WHERE status='running' at ~/.cast/diecast.db. That gap is the finding.

  • The launch predicate is slot-bounded. _dispatcher_loop launches only when COUNT(status='running') < MAX_CONCURRENT_AGENTS (default 7, CAST_MAX_CONCURRENT_AGENTS). At 7 it launches nothing and logs nothing — no error, because nothing failed.
  • The occupants are zombies, not workers. Abandoned interactive Claude sessions idling at a new task? / selection-menu prompt stay status='running' forever: the monitor sees a live tmux session and has no terminal-output contract, so it never marks them terminal. On restart, recover_stale_runs resumes them and re-saturates the slots.
  • The signature is diagnostic. Flat ~16 ms HTTP latency, zero Launched lines across several 10-second dispatcher cycles, and free slots on paper together read as saturation — not a stuck loop.
03

Still unanswered

The analysis ends at diagnosis. Two questions are deliberately left open and are not resolved into a recommendation the data does not make.

  • Open

    Should the monitor auto-reap interactive sessions idle past a threshold, or is operator-driven cancellation the safer default given the risk of killing an actively-working pane?

  • Deferred

    Should the status API's running filter be reconciled to the raw DB count, or is the divergence intentional — with only the docs needing a warning against trusting it?

Run-state investigation · family: data_analysis · diagnosis only
